---
title: "2.1 Mean Reversion and Stationarity"
subtitle: "PDF notes | Chapter 02. The Basics of Mean Reversion"

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    mainfont: "NotoSerifCJKsc-Regular.otf"
    mainfontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=NotoSerifCJKsc-Bold.otf"
    sansfont: "NotoSansCJKsc-Regular.otf"
    sansfontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=NotoSansCJKsc-Bold.otf"
    monofont: "MapleMono-NF-CN-Regular.ttf"
    monofontoptions:
      - "Path=../../../../assets/fonts/"
      - "BoldFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-Bold.ttf"
      - "ItalicFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-Italic.ttf"
      - "BoldItalicFont=MapleMono-NF-CN-BoldItalic.ttf"
    mathfont: "latinmodern-math.otf"
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    filecolor: blue
include-in-header:
  text: |
    \addtokomafont{disposition}{\rmfamily\bfseries}
    \addtokomafont{title}{\rmfamily\bfseries}
    \addtokomafont{author}{\rmfamily}
    \addtokomafont{date}{\rmfamily}
---
# Mean Reversion Here Means Time-Series Mean Reversion

This chapter is about time-series mean reversion: the price of one asset, or of
one constructed portfolio, tends to move back toward a mean determined by its
own history. That is different from cross-sectional mean reversion, where the
relative returns of assets inside a basket revert toward the basket return. Chan
defers that stock-basket setting to Chapter 4.

The practical question in this section is narrower and more technical. Given a
price series $y(t)$, can we show that its current level contains information
about the next move, and can we show that the pullback happens fast enough to
support a trading strategy?

# Mean Reversion and Stationarity Are Equivalent Views of One Property

Chan presents two mathematical descriptions of the same phenomenon.

The mean-reversion description says that the next price change is related to the
gap between current price and mean price. If current price is above the mean,
the next move should tend to be downward. If current price is below the mean,
the next move should tend to be upward.

The stationarity description says that the variance of the log price grows more
slowly than it would for a geometric random walk. A random walk diffuses
normally: uncertainty grows linearly with elapsed time. A stationary series can
still wander, but its uncertainty grows sublinearly.

These views lead to different tests:

- the ADF framework tests the first description directly
- the Hurst exponent and Variance Ratio framework test the second description

# The ADF Regression Encodes Pullback Explicitly

Chan writes the discrete-time model as:

$$
\Delta y(t) = \lambda y(t-1) + \mu + \beta t
  + \alpha_1 \Delta y(t-1) + \cdots + \alpha_k \Delta y(t-k) + \varepsilon_t
\tag{2.1}
$$

where

$$
\Delta y(t) \equiv y(t) - y(t-1).
$$

This equation is easy to interpret term by term:

- $\lambda y(t-1)$ is the key mean-reversion term
- $\mu$ allows a nonzero long-run mean
- $\beta t$ allows deterministic drift
- lagged differences $\alpha_i \Delta y(t-i)$ absorb short-run serial
  correlation in changes

The null hypothesis in the ADF test is $\lambda = 0$. Under that null, the next
change does not depend on current level, so the series behaves like a unit-root
process rather than a mean-reverting one.

For a trader, the sign of $\lambda$ matters immediately:

- $\lambda < 0$: high levels tend to be followed by downward moves, and low
  levels by upward moves
- $\lambda = 0$: no pullback force is detected
- $\lambda > 0$: the series is not mean reverting at all

The ADF test statistic is the estimated coefficient divided by its standard
error:

$$
\frac{\hat{\lambda}}{\operatorname{SE}(\hat{\lambda})}.
$$

To reject the null in favor of mean reversion, this statistic must be negative
enough relative to Dickey-Fuller critical values. Chan also follows the
practical simplification $\beta = 0$: any deterministic drift is usually much
smaller than day-to-day fluctuations, so the drift term is typically not the
economically important piece.

# The USD.CAD Example Shows the Difference Between Sign and Significance

Chan applies the ADF test to the daily USD.CAD exchange rate sampled at
17:00 ET. The example is useful because it shows that "negative" and
"statistically convincing" are not the same thing.

The reported result is approximately:

- ADF statistic: $-1.84$
- 10 percent critical value: $-2.594$

Because $-1.84$ is not more negative than $-2.594$, the test does not reject
the null that $\lambda = 0$ even at the 90 percent level. The series therefore
does not qualify as stationary by this strict test.

But the sign is still informative. The estimate is negative, which suggests the
series is at least not obviously trending. This distinction becomes important
later when Chan shifts from pure hypothesis testing to half-life.

# Stationarity Can Also Be Described Through Diffusion Speed

The Hurst-exponent route starts from log prices:

$$
z(t) = \log y(t).
$$

Define the variance of the increment over lag $\tau$ as

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\tau)
= \left\langle |z(t+\tau)-z(t)|^2 \right\rangle.
\tag{2.2}
$$

For a geometric random walk, this variance grows linearly:

$$
\left\langle |z(t+\tau)-z(t)|^2 \right\rangle \sim \tau.
\tag{2.3}
$$

For a more general process, Chan writes

$$
\left\langle |z(t+\tau)-z(t)|^2 \right\rangle \sim \tau^{2H},
\tag{2.4}
$$

where $H$ is the Hurst exponent.

This gives a compact classification rule:

- $H = 0.5$: geometric random walk
- $H < 0.5$: mean reverting
- $H > 0.5$: trending

The smaller $H$ is, the stronger the pullback tendency. But Chan is careful
about interpretation: stationarity here does not mean prices are perfectly range
bound with time-independent variance. It means diffusion is slower than normal,
not absent.

# The Variance Ratio Test Supplies the Missing Hypothesis Test

An estimated Hurst exponent is only a point estimate. A value such as $H=0.49$
may suggest weak mean reversion, but finite samples can easily produce values
slightly below or above $0.5$ by chance.

The Variance Ratio test asks whether the diffusion behavior is statistically
distinguishable from the random-walk benchmark. Conceptually, it tests whether
long-lag variance scales like one-period variance in the way a random walk would
imply. In Chan's USD.CAD example, the Variance Ratio test returns a p-value of
about $0.367$, so the random-walk null still cannot be rejected.

That creates a useful tension:

- ADF does not reject unit root
- Hurst estimate is close to, but slightly below, 0.5
- Variance Ratio also fails to reject random walk

The lesson is that the series may be weakly mean reverting in sign, yet not
statistically strong enough to pass a demanding academic threshold.

# Half-Life Converts the Econometric Coefficient into Trading Time

Chan's next move is the most useful one for trading. He asks not only whether
$\lambda$ is significantly negative, but whether it is negative enough to make a
strategy commercially worthwhile.

Ignoring the deterministic drift and lagged-difference terms in Equation 2.1
leads to the continuous-time approximation

$$
dy(t) = (\lambda y(t-1) + \mu)\,dt + d\varepsilon.
\tag{2.5}
$$

This is the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck form for a mean-reverting process. Its expected
solution is

$$
\mathbb{E}[y(t)]
= y_0 e^{\lambda t} - \frac{\mu}{\lambda}(1-e^{\lambda t}).
\tag{2.6}
$$

Because mean reversion requires $\lambda < 0$, the transient component
$y_0 e^{\lambda t}$ decays exponentially. The half-life is therefore

$$
\text{half-life} = -\frac{\log 2}{\lambda}.
$$

This formula matters for three reasons:

1. if $\lambda > 0$, the series is not mean reverting
2. if $\lambda < 0$ but close to zero, half-life becomes very long
3. half-life sets a natural time scale for signal construction

Chan explicitly warns against using a lookback much shorter than the reversion
time scale. If half-life is roughly 20 days, a 5-day moving average is usually
too short to represent the equilibrium the strategy claims to trade.

# The USD.CAD Half-Life Example Explains Why Failure to Reject Is Not the End

Chan estimates $\lambda$ for USD.CAD by regressing $\Delta y(t)$ on $y(t-1)$.
The reported half-life is about 115 days.

This is a useful example precisely because it is awkward:

- the series did not pass strict stationarity tests
- but the estimated pullback coefficient is still negative
- and the half-life is finite, though long

The conclusion is not that USD.CAD is an ideal candidate. The conclusion is
that strict rejection at the 90 or 95 percent level is not the only criterion a
trader should care about. The trading horizon, turnover, and capital lock-up all
depend on the half-life.

# The Linear Strategy Uses Z-Score as the Position Rule

Once a price series has a usable half-life, Chan proposes a deliberately simple
linear strategy. Let the moving mean and moving standard deviation be computed
over a lookback near the half-life. Define the Z-score as

$$
z_t
= \frac{y_t - \operatorname{movingAvg}(y_t)}
       {\operatorname{movingStd}(y_t)}.
$$

Then the position is set proportional to the negative Z-score:

$$
\text{position}_t \propto -z_t.
$$

This means:

- above-mean prices create short exposure
- below-mean prices create long exposure
- larger deviations create larger positions

Chan calls this "linear" because the number of units changes linearly with the
normalized deviation. It is not necessarily practical in raw form because it
can imply constant rebalancing and, without constraints, very large exposure.
But as a teaching device it is powerful because it uses almost no free
parameters.

# Why Moving Averages and Moving Standard Deviations Still Matter

An obvious objection is that a stationary process should already have a fixed
mean and standard deviation, so why estimate them with moving windows at all?

Chan's answer is pragmatic. Even if a price series is approximately stationary,
its effective mean can drift slowly as economic conditions, policy regimes, or
management decisions change. Its variance can also evolve with time. Using
moving estimates allows the strategy to adapt to that slow evolution while still
trading reversion over a shorter horizon.

# Jupyter Lab Makes the Tests Easier to Inspect Than Static Equations Alone

This section becomes much clearer when the tests are run interactively. The
`notebooks/` folder packages the main ideas into small, separate experiments:

- `adf-test.ipynb` tests whether current price level helps predict the next move
- `variance-ratio-test.ipynb` checks whether diffusion still looks like a
  random walk
- `half-life.ipynb` converts the estimated pullback coefficient into trading
  time

From this section directory, the practical workflow is:

```bash
jupyter lab
```

Then open the notebooks one by one and read them in the same order as the
chapter's logic:

1. start with ADF to ask whether pullback exists at all
2. compare that answer with Variance Ratio evidence
3. finish with half-life to decide whether the reversion is fast enough to
   matter

Jupyter Lab is useful here not because it changes the mathematics, but because
it lets the learner inspect intermediate outputs instead of only final
statistics. You can see the regression inputs, sample windows, test statistics,
and plots in one workspace, then modify the asset, date range, or lookback and
watch the conclusion change. That makes the chapter's main lesson more concrete:
mean reversion is not a slogan, but a sequence of testable claims.

# Preliminary Statistical Tests Can Be More Powerful Than a Backtest

Chan ends the section by explaining why these derivations are worth the effort.
A direct backtest of one strategy generates statistics from completed trades
only. A stationarity or half-life analysis uses almost every bar of the price
history.

That difference matters statistically. The preliminary tests may have more power
than the Sharpe ratio of any single prototype strategy because they consume more
observations and are not tied to one particular implementation choice. They do
not replace backtesting, but they help decide whether a mean-reversion search is
worth pursuing before the researcher starts tuning a trading rule.

# Review Checklist

- Does the current level enter the next-change equation with a plausibly
  negative coefficient?
- Is the negative coefficient statistically convincing under ADF or only weakly
  suggestive?
- Do Hurst and Variance Ratio evidence support the same qualitative reading?
- What half-life does the estimated $\lambda$ imply?
- Is the intended lookback aligned with that half-life rather than chosen by
  brute-force search alone?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [1.4 When Not to Backtest a Strategy](../../../chapter-01/sections/04-when-not-to-backtest-a-strategy/pdf-notes.qmd)
- Next: [2.2 Cointegration](../02-cointegration/pdf-notes.qmd)
